Original Text:
Cars. Cars have been around since they became famous in the 1900s, when Henry Ford created and built the first ModelT. Cars have played a major role in our every day lives since then. But now, people are starting to question if limiting car usage would be a good thing. To me, limiting the use of cars might be a good thing to do.

In like matter of this, article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," by Elizabeth Rosenthal states, how automobiles are the linchpin of suburbs, where middle class families from either Shanghai or Chicago tend to make their homes. Experts say how this is a huge impediment to current efforts to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from tailpipe. Passenger cars are responsible for 12 percent of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe...and up to 50 percent in some carintensive areas in the United States. Cars are the main reason for the greenhouse gas emissions because of a lot of people driving them around all the time getting where they need to go. Article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," by Robert Duffer says, how Paris, after days of nearrecord pollution, enforced a partial driving ban to clear the air of the global city. It also says, how on Monday, motorist with evennumbered license plates were ordered to leave their cars at home or be fined a 22euro fine 31. The same order would be applied to oddnumbered plates the following day. Cars are the reason for polluting entire cities like Paris. This shows how bad cars can be because, of all the pollution that they can cause to an entire city.

Likewise, in the article, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," by Andrew Selsky says, how programs that's set to spread to other countries, millions of Columbians hiked, biked, skated, or took the bus to work during a carfree day, leaving streets of this capital city eerily devoid of traffic jams. It was the third straight year cars have been banned with only buses and taxis permitted for the Day Without Cars in the capital city of 7 million. People like the idea of having carfree days because, it allows them to lesson the pollution that cars put out of their exhaust from people driving all the time. The article also tells how parks and sports centers have bustled throughout the city uneven, pitted sidewalks have been replaced by broad, smooth sidewalks rushhour restrictions have dramatically cut traffic and new restaurants and upscale shopping districts have cropped up. Having no cars has been good for the country of Columbia because, it has aloud them to repair things that have needed repairs for a long time, traffic jams have gone down, and restaurants and shopping districts have popped up, all due to the fact of having less cars around.

In conclusion, the use of less cars and having carfree days, have had a big impact on the environment of cities because, it is cutting down the air pollution that the cars have majorly polluted, it has aloud countries like Columbia to repair sidewalks, and cut down traffic jams. Limiting the use of cars would be a good thing for America. So we should limit the use of cars by maybe riding a bike, or maybe walking somewhere that isn't that far from you and doesn't need the use of a car to get you there. To me, limiting the use of cars might be a good thing to do.
Rewritten Text:
Since their popularization in the 1900s, marked by Henry Ford's creation of the Model T, automobiles have been integral to our daily routines. However, the potential benefits of restricting car usage are now being considered. Personally, I believe that limiting car use could be a positive step.

Elizabeth Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," points out that cars form the backbone of suburban life, where many middle-class families in places like Shanghai or Chicago reside. Experts note that this dependence significantly hinders efforts to lower greenhouse gas emissions from vehicles. Across Europe, passenger cars account for 12 percent of such emissions, a figure that can reach 50 percent in car-dependent regions of the United States. The frequency of car usage is a primary contributor to these greenhouse gas emissions. In a related instance, Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," details how Paris implemented a partial driving ban after experiencing days of record-high pollution levels. On the specified Monday, drivers with even-numbered license plates faced fines for using their vehicles, with the same restriction applying to odd-numbered plates the following day. Cars are largely responsible for the extensive pollution affecting cities like Paris, highlighting the detrimental environmental impact they can have.

Similarly, Andrew Selsky's article, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," describes how millions of Colombians participated in a car-free day by walking, biking, skating, or using public transportation, resulting in unusually quiet streets in the capital city. This annual event, in its third year, banned private vehicles, allowing only buses and taxis within the city of seven million residents. Car-free days are popular because they reduce pollution generated by car exhaust. The article further mentions improvements such as upgraded parks and sports centers, replaced sidewalks, reduced rush-hour congestion, and the emergence of new restaurants and shopping areas. In conclusion, decreased car usage and car-free initiatives have considerably influenced the environmental conditions of cities, notably by diminishing air pollution. These practices have also enabled countries like Columbia to improve infrastructure and alleviate traffic congestion. Therefore, restricting car usage could be beneficial for America. We might consider alternatives like cycling or walking for shorter distances to reduce our reliance on cars. To me, limiting the use of cars might be a good thing to do.

Original Text:
Transportation is a large necessity in most countries worldwide. With no doubt, cars, buses, and other means of transportation make going from place to place easier and faster. However there's always a negative pollution. Although mobile transportation are a huge part of daily lives, we are endangering the Earth with harmful greenhouse gases, which could be suppressed.

A small suburb community in Germany called Vauban, has started a "carfree" lifestyle. In this city, markets and stores are placed nearby homes, instead of being located by farend highways. Although Vauban is not completely carfree, 70% of Vauban families do not own cars Even a large 57% of families stated to have sold their cars to move to Vauban. Some families have even said to be less stressed depending on car transportation. Cars are responsible for about 12% of greenhouse gases, and can even be up to 50% in some carintensive areas in the United States.

Another insight to reduced car zones brings Paris' incident with smog. Paris' officials created a system that would in fact lower smog rates. On Monday, the motorists with evennumbered license plates numbers would be ordered to leave their cars at home, or they would suffer a fine. Same rule would occur on Tuesday, except motorists with oddnumbered license plates were targeted with fines. Congestion, or traffic, was reduced by 60% after five days of intense smog. Diesel fuel played a huge part in this pollution, having the fact that 67% of vehicles in France are of Diesel fuel. The impact of the clearing of smog, resided in banning the Tuesday rule of odd license plates.

Could you imagine a day without seeing a single car being used? This phenomenon occurs once a year in Bogota, Colombia. With the exception of buses and taxis being used, cars are to be left unattended for an entire day. Having a carfree day just once a year can even reduce the pollution slightly. The day without cars is part of a campaign that originated in Bogota in the mid 1990s. This campaign has renewed and constructed numerous bicycle paths and sidewalks all over the city. Parks and sports centers have also sprung from this campaign. Devoting your time to a carfree lifestyle has it's hassles, but in hindsight, it has it's benefits.

To conclude, living a carfree lifestyle does not seem like a possibility in this day and age, however managing the use of cars and pollution is something every country should take time investing in. Think about how much of an impact it would be if everywhere worldwide would take part in airpollution reduction. Mobile transportation is lifestyle in a sense, and being dependent on cars or other means of transportation can impact the health of the Earth and even ourselves.
Rewritten Text:
In many nations across the globe, transportation is a significant necessity. Cars, buses, and other vehicles undeniably simplify and expedite travel. However, this convenience comes with the drawback of pollution. While mobile transportation is deeply woven into daily life, our reliance on it contributes to harmful greenhouse gas emissions that could potentially be minimized.

The small German suburb of Vauban has adopted a "car-free" approach to living. In this community, essential services like markets and stores are situated close to residences, rather than along distant highways. Although not entirely devoid of cars, a significant 70% of Vauban's families choose not to own vehicles. Remarkably, 57% of families reported selling their cars specifically to relocate to Vauban. Some residents even express feeling less stressed by minimizing their dependence on cars. It's worth noting that automobiles contribute around 12% of global greenhouse gas emissions, and this figure can soar to 50% in car-dependent areas within the United States.

The city of Paris provides another interesting perspective on the benefits of reduced car usage, particularly in relation to smog reduction. Faced with a severe smog event, Parisian authorities implemented a system designed to lower pollution levels. On Mondays, vehicles with even-numbered license plates were prohibited from use, with violators facing fines. The same regulation applied on Tuesdays, but targeting vehicles with odd-numbered license plates. This initiative resulted in a remarkable 60% reduction in congestion, or traffic, within just five days. Diesel fuel played a considerable role in the pollution problem, considering that 67% of vehicles in France utilize diesel. However, the positive impact of clearing the smog was compromised when the Tuesday ban on odd-numbered plates was discontinued.

Can you picture a day where cars are completely absent? This becomes a reality once a year in Bogota, Colombia. With the exception of buses and taxis, private vehicles are parked for the entire day. Even a single car-free day can make a small dent in overall pollution. This annual event is part of a larger campaign that began in Bogota in the mid-1990s. The campaign has prompted the creation and improvement of numerous bicycle paths and sidewalks throughout the city. Parks and recreational centers have also emerged as a result. While committing to a car-free lifestyle may present challenges, the potential benefits are clear.

In conclusion, completely abandoning cars might seem impractical in today's society. However, managing car usage and reducing pollution should be a priority for all countries. Consider the significant impact if nations worldwide collectively prioritized air pollution reduction. Mobile transportation is deeply ingrained in our way of life. However, over-reliance on cars and other vehicles can have negative consequences for both the health of the planet and our own well-being.

Rewritten Text:
How often do you find yourself driving? Do you use a car or other motorized vehicle to commute to your job, run errands at the store, or visit the mall? Have you ever considered just how many individuals worldwide are doing the same – relying on fuel-powered vehicles for every journey? It's easy to overlook the significant environmental harm caused each time you start your engine. What if you chose not to drive to work today? For many habitual drivers, the immediate reaction might be, "Impossible! I can't function without my car!" Yet, living with less reliance on personal vehicles is achievable. Cities like Havana, Paris, and Bogotá have each implemented strategies to limit car usage among their populations, often with positive results. Reducing your dependence on a car can, in turn, generate various advantages and benefits for both you and the wider community.

One less obvious advantage of reducing your driving is the positive impact on your well-being. As Heidrun Walter notes, "He was always tense when he had a car. He's much happier without it." Reflect on the potential for road rage that driving often entails. Surely, this does not contribute to a positive state of mind or good health. The act of driving demands considerable concentration and mental energy – navigating routes, dealing with inconsiderate drivers, and so on – which can quickly lead to stress and fatigue. Highly populated cities such as New York and Paris often experience traffic congestion and high levels of pollution. According to Robert Duffer in "Paris bans driving due to smog," layers of warm air can trap vehicle emissions. This is hardly conducive to good health. Duffer also reports that Paris was forced to impose a temporary driving ban when pollution levels reached unprecedented highs. After just a few days of reduced driving, pollution levels dropped considerably. When driving is limited, people seek alternative modes of transport, such as walking, cycling, or skating. These activities all provide physical exercise. You get a workout and feel mentally rejuvenated compared to simply sitting in a car.

Decreasing car usage can also enhance the aesthetic quality of your city. Pollution is detrimental to plant life and creates unpleasant odors. It casts a metaphorical pall over the urban landscape, creating a "dirty" atmosphere. Reduced driving translates to lower emissions of harmful pollutants, resulting in a cleaner environment. In her article, "In German Suburb, Life goes on Without Cars," Elisabeth Rosenthal highlights that limiting car and motor vehicle traffic naturally leads to more pedestrians. Increased foot traffic necessitates more pedestrian pathways and conveniently located stores within walking distance. As Andrew Selsky observes, "Parks and sports centers have bloomed throughout the city uneven pitted sidewalks have been replaced by broad, smooth sidewalks... and new restaurants and upscale shopping districts have cropped up." As previously mentioned, pollution harms the environment. Organizations like the Environmental Protection Agency in the U.S. are promoting "car-reduced" communities, according to Rosenthal. These communities experience significantly lower levels of pollution and are cleaner overall. Cities are also embracing this concept, organizing "no-car driving" days. Bogotá, Colombia, hosts an annual "car-free" day where only buses and taxis are permitted; other drivers face fines. While fining individuals for using their own property may be contentious, the event has seen successful participation and a noticeable reduction in smog. In conclusion, while the idea of reducing our reliance on cars in favor of walking may seem impractical, it is not impossible. Reducing car usage offers numerous benefits and advantages in our lives. These include improved health, more attractive cities, and a better environment for everyone.

Rewritten Text:
Cars are a truly remarkable invention, arguably one of the most significant technological advancements in history. They provide us with transportation, enabling us to move swiftly from one location to another. Isn't that precisely what we desire? As humans, we aim to travel between places as quickly as possible, and cars effectively fulfill this need, allowing us to traverse cities in mere minutes—a speed unmatched by most other means. While trains and public buses offer alternative transportation options, cars often seem like the superior choice. However, despite their undeniable advantages, cars might also contribute to excessive stress and potentially harm our environment in unforeseen ways. Opting for buses or trains eliminates concerns about car maintenance and the frustrations of traffic congestion. Moreover, these alternatives generally produce less air pollution. While it may not be immediately apparent, limiting car usage offers numerous benefits.

One such advantage, benefiting not only humans but also plants and animals, is the reduction of air pollution. Currently, cars emit harmful gases into the atmosphere, known as greenhouse gases, which are released through exhaust pipes. While the emissions from a single car may seem insignificant, the collective impact of all cars is substantial, particularly in densely populated cities like Paris. In fact, Paris experienced record-breaking pollution levels, prompting authorities to implement temporary driving restrictions. As Robert Duffer reports in the article "Paris Bans Driving Due to Smog," motorists with even-numbered license plates were required to refrain from driving or face a fine, a rule that would alternate daily with odd-numbered plates. These restrictions led to a remarkable 60% reduction in traffic congestion in the French capital. Similarly, Bogota, Colombia, has embraced car-free days to combat smog, as highlighted by Andrew Selsky in "Carfree Day Is Spinning into a Big Hit in Bogota," which emphasizes the initiative's goal of promoting alternative transportation and lowering pollution. Across the globe, people are increasingly recognizing the environmental benefits of reducing car dependence.

Another significant advantage of limiting car use is the reduction of stress. Driving is widely recognized as a source of considerable stress. Traffic jams, the constant need for car washes, and the cacophony of noise within the car all contribute to heightened stress levels. Reducing car usage would alleviate these stressors, eliminating traffic congestion, car maintenance hassles, and cramped, noisy commutes. In the article "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," Elisabeth Rosenthal quotes a resident named Hartmut Walter, who states, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." Walter's experience suggests that reducing reliance on cars can lead to increased happiness and reduced stress. Similarly, in "Carfree Day Is Spinning into a Big Hit in Bogota," Andrew Selsky notes that these days offer "a good opportunity to take away stress..." If we have the option to reduce stress, it's an opportunity worth seizing. Lower stress levels translate to a higher quality of life.

In conclusion, limiting car use offers dual advantages: it benefits the environment and reduces stress. Considering the existing environmental challenges, actively working to restore the environment to a healthier state is crucial. Furthermore, given the proven link between stress and impaired personal health, minimizing stress is vital for well-being. If the goal is to improve the environment and reduce stress, then embracing this advantage is essential. While some may not immediately recognize the benefits, limiting car use has proven to be effective in improving environmental conditions and reducing stress in multiple locations. This approach provides a potent advantage with far-reaching positive effects.

Rewritten Text:
Dear State Senator, with the utmost respect, I am writing to request a reevaluation of our presidential election process. I believe transitioning away from the electoral college to a system that more accurately reflects the popular will is crucial. The electoral college, as it stands, presents an inequitable system where individual electors can potentially override the collective voice of the American people.

While revising the electoral college may present perceived drawbacks, the potential benefits outweigh the risks. A direct popular vote system might, for example, open the door to candidates attempting to directly incentivize voters with promises of financial gain. Furthermore, the complexities of a direct democracy, such as resolving a tied election and ensuring a fair tie-breaking mechanism, need careful consideration. Therefore, complete removal of the electoral college may not be the answer, but reform is undeniably necessary.

The current system grants electors undue power in determining the election's outcome. The winner-take-all approach inherent in the electoral college unfairly disadvantages voters, as candidates often concentrate their efforts solely on so-called "swing" states. For instance, during the 2000 presidential campaign, candidates completely overlooked seventeen states. Given these shortcomings, the electoral college has become an outdated mechanism and demands either replacement or significant revision.

Presidential candidates assemble a slate of electors during their campaigns. These electors allow candidates to secure votes without campaigning in every single state. In essence, the president's primary task is simply to await the results. In fact, a candidate could conceivably win the presidency without extensive campaigning, as the electors' votes ultimately determine the outcome. This creates a scenario where candidates might attempt to sway electors in key states through bribery, securing that state's vote.

Voters technically cast their ballots for a slate of electors, rather than directly for the candidates themselves. These electors are chosen through various methods, including state conventions, state party central committees, and sometimes by the presidential candidates themselves. A major cause for concern with the electoral college lies in what is known as the "disaster factor." This refers to potential systemic vulnerabilities, such as the incident in 1960 when segregationists within the Louisiana legislature nearly replaced Democratic electors with individuals opposed to John F. Kennedy.

In conclusion, the electoral college is an antiquated method of conducting elections. This system concentrates power in the hands of individual electors rather than empowering the people. Established during a time when educational standards were far below current levels, the practice of electing representatives who then choose the president is no longer appropriate for our modern democracy.

Rewritten Text:
My fellow citizens, automobiles have become deeply integrated into our everyday existence. While they offer numerous benefits, there are clear advantages to curtailing their use. Restricting car usage can lead to improved environmental health and significant cost savings. Let me elaborate on why thoughtfully limiting car dependence is a wise decision.

For example, Paris, as reported in Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," devised a clever partial driving ban to combat the city's air pollution. The initiative involved alternating days when only vehicles with even or odd-numbered license plates were permitted to drive, with fines imposed on violators. Concurrently, public transportation was made free for the entire week. This strategy effectively reduced congestion and smog, benefiting the environment by lowering pollution levels. Additionally, it provided a more economical means of commuting. The article states "Congestion was down 60 percent in the capital of France, after five days of intensifying smog...rivaled Beijing, China, which is known as one of the most polluted cities in the world". This demonstrates the severity of the smog issue, and how limiting car usage produced tremendous change.

Conversely, in Vauban, Germany, residents have taken a more extreme approach, relinquishing car ownership altogether. According to Elisabeth Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb, life goes on without cars," approximately 70% of residents no longer own cars due to the expense of keeping one on the outskirts of town and the general prohibition within this experimental new district. They express satisfaction with their car-free lifestyle. "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way" said Heidrun Walter, a resident in Vauban according to the article. Generational trends show a decline in car usage and license ownership, particularly among younger demographics, as older individuals are more likely to maintain their licenses. As outlined in Elisabeth Rosenthal's article "The end of car culture," cars are increasingly viewed as a simple means of transportation, with less emphasis placed on the vehicle itself and more on efficient movement from one place to another.

Ultimately, cars serve as a mode of transportation, but various alternative options exist that are either free or more affordable, such as buses, trains, bicycles, or carpooling. These alternatives simultaneously reduce pollution and congestion, promoting better environmental health.

Rewritten Text:
The electoral college has officially been deemed unfair, outdated, and irrational, according to Plumer (Source 2). This sentiment is shared by many, who view the electoral college as a potential disaster or simply a flawed system. While some arguments exist for retaining the electoral college, such as avoiding runoff elections or giving weight to larger states, the opposition outweighs the support. Those who disapprove of the electoral college generally advocate for a popular vote system, considering it the superior option.

One reason the electoral college is perceived as unfair is that not all individual votes are equally valued; the decisions of electors hold greater weight. In a popular vote election, every person's vote would count, rather than prioritizing the choices of a select group holding authority. Furthermore, electors could potentially be influenced or even bribed, undermining the democratic process.

Many believe the electoral college is an outdated system that no longer suits the times. Plumer (Source 2) quotes Bob Dole: "Abolish the electoral college!" Many agree with this sentiment, suggesting that a direct vote would be preferable to allowing a select group to determine the outcome. Some even jokingly suggest that even a less sophisticated system could yield better results. According to Plumer (Source 2), ".....over 60 percent of voters would prefer a direct election to the kind we have now," a figure that has steadily increased since the data was recorded in 2000.

The electoral college is also seen as irrational. Its structure, involving electors who ultimately choose the president rather than a direct popular vote, is confusing and convoluted. As Plumer (Source 2) explains: "Under the electoral college system, voters vote not for the president, but for a slate of electors, who in turn elect the president........Who are the electors? They can be anyone not holding public office. Who picks the electors in the first place? It depends on the state. Sometimes state conventions, sometimes the state party's central committee, sometimes the presidential candidate themselves. Can voters control whom their electors vote for? Not always. DO voters sometimes get confused about the electors and vote for the wrong candiate? Sometimes." This complex system raises questions about voter control and the potential for confusion.

While it's acknowledged that the electoral college might offer some benefits, such as stability, the perceived problems outweigh the advantages. The simpler, and more equitable solution, in the eyes of many, is to adopt a popular vote system. As stated earlier, "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational" (Plumer, Source 2).

Rewritten Text:
Established by the founding fathers and enshrined in the Constitution, the Electoral College has been in place for centuries. I believe this system should be preserved; it has functioned adequately thus far, and I see no compelling justification for its elimination. The continued use of the Electoral College is warranted because it offers benefits in at least five significant scenarios.

To some extent, the Electoral College presents potential risks unless electors remain completely loyal to their party and resist the temptation to exploit their voting power. Essentially, citizens are voting for a group of electors who then choose the president. These electors could disregard the popular vote and instead vote according to their own preferences. Furthermore, it's conceivable that a state might submit two conflicting slates of electors. While a tie would be highly improbable in a direct popular vote, the Electoral College always carries that possibility. In some states, candidates may not even campaign extensively due to the overwhelming dominance of a single party, a situation exacerbated by the "winner-takes-all" system. A direct vote system might incentivize candidates to actively campaign in all states to maximize their vote count. However, many arguments against the Electoral College focus on exceptional circumstances, so considering the opposing viewpoint is important.

Swing states effectively determine the outcome of presidential elections and, as such, their voters tend to be the most informed. Knowing that their votes will be decisive, they pay closer attention to news coverage and campaign events. Candidates dedicate significant resources to these swing states, seeking to win them over. Many states, dominated by one party or the other, are predictable before the election even begins. Voters in swing states are more likely to carefully consider the candidates and their platforms, making them arguably best suited to choose the nation's leader.

The Electoral College can prevent the need for runoff elections by alleviating pressure that might complicate the election results. If the United States were to adopt a direct vote system, for instance, and the results were closely contested, the absence of the Electoral College safeguard could lead to significant confusion. Instances like Nixon in 1968 and Clinton in 1992, who both won the presidency despite securing only a 43% plurality of the popular vote, underscore this point.

It's understandable that minority voters in states with strong partisan leanings might find the Electoral College frustrating. These voters might be less engaged in campaigns, believing their individual votes won't significantly impact the state's outcome. However, one person cannot determine an election on their own, and it should be noted that not every member of the population voted in the 2012 election. For most, expressing their preference is valued more than deciding the entire election. Becoming educated and informed about the government increases the likelihood of one's vote having a meaningful impact.

Rewritten Text:
Dear Senator,

I urge you to preserve the Electoral College. This system comprises 538 electors, and securing the presidency requires a majority of 270. Each state appoints its own electors, chosen by the respective political parties of the candidates. Maintaining the Electoral College is vital as it ensures a decisive outcome and guarantees that the president represents all citizens, not just a select few.

One compelling reason to retain the Electoral College is the assurance of a clear result in favor of one candidate. While a tie in the national electoral vote is theoretically possible, its occurrence is highly improbable, despite the 538 electors constituting an even number. The 2012 election, for instance, saw Obama receiving 61.7 percent of the electoral votes despite winning 51.3 percent of the popular vote. This discrepancy arises because states generally award electoral votes on a winner-take-all basis, meaning that even a narrow plurality in a state can lead to a substantial electoral vote victory. However, this winner-take-all system results in candidates concentrating their efforts on closely contested "swing" states, neglecting states where their chances of winning are minimal. Nevertheless, the winning candidate's proportion of the Electoral College vote consistently surpasses their proportion of the national popular vote.

Another crucial reason to uphold the Electoral College is the principle that the president should represent everyone. The Electoral College mandates that a presidential candidate possess broad, transregional appeal. No single region possesses sufficient electoral votes to elect a president on its own. Consequently, a candidate with strong regional support, such as Rodney in the South, lacks the motivation to campaign intensively in states they are certain to win, as increasing their margin in those states yields no additional electoral votes. A president lacking widespread appeal is unlikely to govern effectively. Residents of other regions may feel disenfranchised, believing their votes are insignificant or that the president does not truly represent them.

In summary, maintaining the Electoral College is essential due to its capacity to prevent electoral ties and its role in ensuring the president represents all Americans.

Dear Senator, I've been considering the optimal method for electing the President and their administration, and I've reached a conclusion. We have two longstanding systems: the Electoral College and election by popular vote. After careful thought, one emerges as preferable to the other in my view.

The Electoral College involves electors convening and casting votes for the President and Vice President. This body consists of 538 individuals who ultimately decide the election. Personally, I find this system flawed. While we believe we are directly voting for the president, we are actually voting for a slate of electors who then cast the deciding vote. Why should a select few, out of the millions of Americans, hold the power to choose the president while the rest of us simply await the outcome? It feels inequitable that only their voices truly matter when electing someone with the power to shape our nation. This is a winner-take-all system, which is not a good thing.

Election by popular vote, on the other hand, grants each citizen the right to directly vote for the President and their chosen officials. It allows us to have a direct say in who will lead and potentially improve our country. Even if our preferred candidate doesn't win, our voice is still registered, reflecting our vision for a better nation. The Electoral College may provide a more definitive result, but knowing our vote truly influences the outcome could encourage greater civic engagement and more thoughtful voting. It empowers us to express our preferences directly, rather than relying on the uncertain influence of a single electoral vote.

Admittedly, the Electoral College has some potential advantages. It can ensure a clear and decisive election outcome. It could be argued that choosing electors is functionally similar to voting directly. However, these limited benefits pale in comparison to the importance of direct participation through a popular vote.

Ultimately, when considering who should be president, it's crucial to reflect on how you want your voice to be heard—through your own direct vote or indirectly through the vote of another. For me, the choice is clear. I favor the system that amplifies my voice in the democratic process.

Rewritten Text:
The desire to stroll in a park, whether with children or grandchildren, or simply for a pleasant walk, is universal. However, current lifestyles threaten to make this impossible due to pervasive pollution, largely stemming from excessive car use in lieu of buses, walking, or carpooling. Limiting car usage is essential, driven by its considerable expense, its discouragement of walking and public transportation, and its contribution to smog pollution.

Car ownership incurs more costs than initially apparent. In Vauban, Germany, parking is severely restricted, requiring residents to purchase expensive parking spots, some costing forty thousand dollars, in addition to the price of their homes. Bogota, Columbia, penalizes violators of its national car-free day with twenty-five dollar fines. While seemingly minor, these expenses accumulate. In Paris, motorists with even-numbered license plates face twenty-two euro fines on certain days, followed by similar fines for odd-numbered plates the next day, resulting in almost four thousand drivers being penalized. Beyond fines, there are ongoing expenses like gasoline. While wealthier individuals may absorb these costs, the financial strain is significantly greater for average families with multiple children.

Public transportation is often taken for granted. Consider the absence of buses, trains, and subways, and the prospect of driving into a city like New York. On Bogota's car-free day, citizens readily embrace public transit or walking, avoiding the twenty-five dollar fine. According to Mayor Antanas Mockus, participation remained high despite rain. Cities like Cali and Valledupar have emulated Bogota's initiative, drawing enthusiastic observers from municipal authorities in other countries. New York promotes a bike-sharing program that allows for convenient travel around the city. Even if some people deem it uncool, public transportation may be essential to preserving the environment.

The difficulty in seeing stars at night is not due to their absence, but the obscuring effect of smog pollution. After a period of near-record pollution, Paris implemented a partial driving ban to improve air quality. The resulting reduction in smog congestion was only sixty percent in five days, due in part to France's tax policies favoring diesel fuel. Bogota's car-free day offers a respite from pollution, while Vauban's restrictions on car access near homes result in virtually no smog. While challenging, even small changes can contribute to environmental improvement.

The examples of Vauban, with its car-free living, and Bogota, with its car-free days, demonstrate lower pollution levels compared to places like Paris, which requires driving bans to mitigate pollution. The high cost of parking in Vauban and the fines in Bogota encourage the use of public transport and walking. Therefore, limiting car usage is vital due to its cost, its promotion of alternative transportation, and its positive impact on reducing smog pollution.

Dear State Senator, I am writing to advocate for the elimination of the Electoral College, believing that presidential elections should be determined solely by the national popular vote. My position is that the Electoral College is inherently unjust and represents a danger to the principles of the U.S. Constitution.

The Electoral College undermines the democratic foundation established by our nation's founders. Such a system calls into question our claim to be a true democracy. A prime illustration of this occurred in the 2000 election between Al Gore and George W. Bush, where Al Gore won the popular vote, yet the Electoral College awarded the presidency to Bush. As the article "The Indefensible Electoral College" points out, "Over 60% of voters would prefer a direct election to the kind we have now," clearly demonstrating a preference among the majority of Americans for a popular vote system.

Moreover, I contend that the Electoral College presents a significant threat to the U.S. Constitution, specifically to the rights of American citizens. The fundamental understanding is that the people of the United States elect their president; however, when the candidate with the most votes loses due to the Electoral College, the democratic process is undermined. "The Indefensible Electoral College" also raises concerns about potential electoral irregularities: "oh what if a state sends two slates of electors to congress? it happened in hawaii in 1960. Luckily, vice president Richard Nixon, who was presiding over the senate, validated only his opponent's electors." Imagine the chaos and potential for manipulation if a similar situation were to occur again, and the double slate of electors was not caught, leading to an unfair advantage for one candidate. The Electoral College is rife with potential complications.

In summary, the Electoral College should be abolished because it is fundamentally unfair and poses a considerable threat to the U.S. Constitution. I urge you to consider these points as you formulate your own conclusion on this crucial matter.

Dear Senator,

Many question the necessity of the Electoral College, arguing that it subverts direct democracy by having citizens vote for electors rather than their preferred candidate. However, the Electoral College remains a functional system, demonstrable through its assurance of outcome certainty and its promotion of a president for all citizens.

The Electoral College's role in ensuring outcome certainty is a primary reason for its continued relevance. A shift to a popular vote system wouldn't necessarily improve our electoral process, and might even lead to more disputes. Richard A. Posner, in his defense of the Electoral College, emphasizes that the winning candidate's share of the Electoral College vote almost always exceeds their share of the popular vote, decreasing the likelihood of contested results. It's a valid point that Electoral College votes often surpass popular vote totals, suggesting a simpler and more decisive voting process. Furthermore, the Electoral College avoids the potential for ties that could occur in a popular vote system, as exemplified by the hypothetical 1992 election tie between Nixon and Clinton. Overall, the Electoral College provides a greater certainty of outcome due to its typically higher vote totals.

Secondly, the Electoral College helps ensure that the president represents the entire nation. It encourages candidates to build trans-regional support rather than focusing solely on regions that favor them. This prevents a situation where a candidate caters to a single area, neglecting the broader needs of the country. For instance, if the Southern region –hypothetically– predominantly supports Republicans, and a Republican candidate focuses exclusively on Southern interests, it would be unfair to other regions with different priorities. While Bradford Plumer's article criticizes the Electoral College for discouraging candidates from engaging with states they are unlikely to win, it can be argued that these states may have already made their voting decisions. It's inefficient for a Democratic candidate to campaign heavily in Republican-leaning Texas, or for a Republican candidate to focus on Democratic-leaning California, when voters have already made up their minds. Also, many arguments against the Electoral College, like those in Plumer's older article, may no longer be relevant due to political shifts over time. To mount a strong argument against the Electoral College, it's crucial to have current and verifiable data, as situations evolve. The efficacy of the Electoral College can be observed in recent elections. For instance, opinions on the Electoral College in the 2004, 2008, and 2012 elections were often tied to people's sentiments about the events and promises made during those times, as well as factors such as youth voter turnout. Ultimately, the Electoral College ensures a president for all Americans by encouraging a nationwide focus.

In conclusion, the Electoral College should be maintained because it provides outcome certainty and fosters a presidency that represents the entire nation. The certainty of outcome is ensured by the usually higher vote totals in the Electoral College compared to a popular vote system. Furthermore, the Electoral College promotes a president who focuses on the nation as a whole, rather than catering to specific regions. Senator, it is important to preserve the Electoral College, as its effectiveness in recent elections suggests its continuing value and reliability in future elections.

Rewritten Text:
In the past, people relied on walking for transportation, whether for errands, obtaining food, or simply spending time outside with family. This raises the question of why modern society has become so dependent on automobiles. Consequently, numerous places globally are enacting regulations aimed at curbing excessive car use.

Since its invention, the automobile has presented an enduring challenge: its environmental impact. With growing global awareness of this issue, rules and laws are being established to restrict or eliminate car usage. Vauban, a community in Germany, has embraced a car-free lifestyle to benefit the environment, and residents are largely pleased with the change. As Heidrun Walter, a mother of two, stated, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way."

Paris, often referred to as "The City of Love," has prohibited cars to combat its severe smog problem. Remarkably, within just five days of the law's implementation, traffic congestion decreased by 60 percent.

Facing the highest smog levels among European capitals, Paris found this measure essential to prevent the city from being overwhelmed. The ban has demonstrably improved air quality, significantly reducing smog in the area.

Major metropolitan areas like New York City, characterized by narrow streets, experience increased congestion with high car volume. Given the proximity of buildings, walking or biking often proves more efficient than driving. New York's successful bikesharing program reflects this. Similarly, the nationwide carsharing initiative encourages carpooling, where several individuals commute together, reducing the number of vehicles on the road and yielding benefits like decreased carbon dioxide emissions, less traffic, and fuel cost savings.

Bill Ford, Executive Chairman of Ford Motor Company, has envisioned a future where personal car ownership is impractical. He proposed collaborating with the telecommunications sector to develop cities, or a network of cities, where "pedestrians, bicycles, private cars, commercial and public transportation traffic are woven into a connected network to save time, conserve resources, lower emission and improve safety." While completely banning cars globally is not feasible due to their convenience compared to trains or walking, reducing our reliance on them is crucial. Overuse poses a threat of excessive greenhouse gas emissions potentially devastating our planet. Possible solutions include implementing a car-free day worldwide, and exploring alternative transportation methods. For example, biking is an excellent way to exercise while traveling more quickly than walking, offering simultaneous health and transportation benefits.

Rewritten Text:
The Electoral College remains a deeply divisive issue in presidential elections. While some defend it as a functional system, others see it as fundamentally flawed. Ultimately, the Electoral College represents a middle ground between congressional election of the president and a direct popular vote. Its history includes problematic presidential elections, and the potential for future, more severe issues is real. Given America's commitment to democracy, many question why citizens lack complete control over directly electing their president. A superior system is needed, and the Electoral College should be eliminated.

One significant flaw is that voters technically choose electors, who then choose the president. While it seems like the desired candidate is ultimately selected, the system contains problematic aspects. The "winner-take-all" rule exemplifies this. An excerpt from "The Indefensible Electoral College" highlights the system's unfairness to voters. Candidates tend to focus on tight races in "swing" states, neglecting states they deem unwinnable. Essentially, this means candidates prioritize specific states, while ignoring others. The same article points out that during the 2000 campaign, candidates completely bypassed 17 states. These facts illustrate the Electoral College's flaws and inherent unfairness, demanding action.

"The disaster factor" presents another significant problem. Bradford Plumer notes that Americans were fortunate that the 2000 election crisis was not worse, suggesting the system is vulnerable to far greater issues. He cites an instance in Louisiana where legislators nearly replaced Democratic candidates with opponents of John F. Kennedy to manipulate the popular vote. This illustrates the system's potential for corruption if such a maneuver were successful. Other potential problems include states sending conflicting slates of electors to Congress, or an electoral vote tie. Although solutions exist for these scenarios, the existence of these possible problems emphasizes the need for a better, comprehensive system.

Richard A. Posner, among others, advocates for retaining the Electoral College, arguing it balances power between larger and smaller states. He contends it gives smaller states proportionally more electoral votes to offset the influence of larger states. However, this doesn't create balance but instead gives smaller states an advantage over larger ones, shifting the imbalance rather than resolving it. Posner also suggests voters in swing states are more engaged in campaigns. However, Plumer's article refutes this, noting that many states were completely ignored by campaigns, including Rhode Island and South Carolina, and voters in a large number of major media markets did not see any campaign advertising. The evidence is clear: the Electoral College is full of easily exploitable loopholes and is an outdated system that should be replaced.

I maintain my position that the Electoral College should be abolished. The people deserve full control over electing the president, and even if that's not entirely achievable, the current system is demonstrably corrupt. Despite some arguing that the Electoral College works adequately, the evidence suggests otherwise. It needs either significant reform or complete replacement.

Rewritten Text:
Despite widespread calls for its abolishment, the Electoral College possesses more advantages than disadvantages. Its inclusion in the Constitution was deliberate, representing a carefully considered balance between a Congressional election of the President and a direct popular vote by eligible citizens. This system empowers both the national populace to voice their preferences and the elected officials of each state to advocate strongly for their constituents. While often criticized, the Electoral College remains a remarkably effective method for electing the President.

The Electoral College incentivizes presidential candidates to cultivate broad, national appeal. For instance, a candidate popular in the South alone cannot secure victory, as no single region holds enough electoral votes to elect a president. While such a candidate might require minimal campaigning in their stronghold, they would need to invest significant effort in regions where their support is less certain. This is a beneficial outcome, as the public ideally desires a president with widespread approval, thereby enhancing their potential for success in office. Candidates typically concentrate their resources on swing states, allocating less time to states where the outcome is predictable. Consequently, voters in these swing states are more likely to be engaged and informed, recognizing their pivotal role in determining the election's outcome. These voters, on average, tend to be the most engaged and thoughtful, making them well-suited to influence the final result.

Furthermore, the Electoral College should be maintained because it generally reflects the will of the people. Although Al Gore won the popular vote in the 2000 election while George W. Bush secured the electoral vote, such an outcome is rare, not seen since 1888. Each party selects a slate of electors who are entrusted to vote for their nominee, and this trust is rarely broken. Many advocate for replacing the Electoral College with a popular vote system, but doing so might increase the likelihood of disputes, as opposed to what happens with the Electoral College. Additionally, a tie in the national popular vote is conceivable given that the total number of votes is even, although it remains highly improbable, as there has never been such a tie. Also, the Electoral College avoids the problem of elections in which no candidate receives a majority of the voter's cast.

Some argue that the Electoral College discourages voters who feel their candidate has little chance of winning their state, such as Republicans in California. However, each individual vote contributes to a state's overall electoral vote allocation. Moreover, voters in presidential elections are primarily motivated by the desire to express their political preferences rather than the belief that their single vote will definitively decide the election. The Electoral College has functioned effectively for over a century and will continue to do so if the nation remains committed to working within its framework.

Rewritten Text:
The truth is undeniable: the electoral college is an unfair, outdated, and fundamentally irrational system. Its supposed merits are largely unsupported assertions, while the counterarguments against direct elections are flimsy at best. It may be difficult to acknowledge, but Bob Dole was correct: the electoral college should be abolished. The core issue is that even the most carefully constructed defenses of the system are flawed. Many who support the electoral college fail to grasp that electors are not always trustworthy, and simply voting for an elector pledged to a particular presidential candidate offers no guarantee that the elector will actually vote as pledged. Consider the views of figures as diverse as Richard Nixon, Jimmy Carter, Bob Dole, the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, and the AFL-CIO: they all, at some point, advocated for the elimination of the electoral college. The fundamental desire is to move to a system where the President of the United States is chosen by popular vote. Currently, the public is not directly voting for their preferred presidential candidate; they are voting for electors. Furthermore, in the event of a tie, the election is decided by the House of Representatives, a body where citizens are not proportionally represented.

Voting for electors is demonstrably not the same as voting directly for a presidential candidate. Can voters be certain that their electors will vote as intended? Not always. Do voters sometimes misunderstand the role of electors and mistakenly vote for the wrong ones? Sometimes. This entire "electoral college" process is flawed and ineffective. People are consistently misled into believing they are voting directly for the presidential candidate of their choice, but this is simply not always the case. Crucially, voters do not choose who the electors are, so who can guarantee their loyalty, integrity, and commitment to fulfilling the will of the people? As an example, in 1960, segregationists in the Louisiana legislature nearly succeeded in replacing Democratic electors with those opposed to John F. Kennedy, effectively negating a popular vote for Kennedy in the state. The inherent potential for deception and manipulation is undeniable. The electoral college provides an unstable foundation for our country. Indeed, "faithless" electors have on occasion refused to support their party's nominee, instead casting a decisive vote for their own preferred candidate. The possibility of such defection and the inevitable deception is clear. The electoral college is not to be trusted, and the people should have the right to a direct vote.

Moreover, in the event of a tie in the electoral vote, the House of Representatives would be responsible for selecting the president and vice president. However, their method is just as problematic as the electoral college itself. The representation is skewed and undemocratic. Because each state receives only one vote, the single representative from Wyoming, representing 500,000 voters, would wield the same power as the 55 representatives from California, who represent 35 million voters. Is this a reasonable, rational, or intelligent system? Such absurd representation exists here on Earth. Given that many voters split their tickets, voting for one party for president and another for Congress, the House's selection could hardly be expected to reflect the will of the people. And if an electoral tie seems unlikely, consider this: in 1968, a shift of just 41,971 voters would have resulted in a deadlocked election. In 1976, a tie would have occurred if a mere 5,559 voters in Ohio and 3,687 voters in Hawaii had voted differently. The election is often only a handful of swing voters away from potential disaster. People are often told that "voting makes a difference," but given all these misrepresentations, can that statement still be made with integrity? Given the many flaws within the system, it's surprising the Electoral College is still afloat. The majority of citizens favor switching the method of electing the President of the United States to a popular vote system.

In conclusion, transitioning from the current electoral college system to a popular vote for the President of the United States would be a significant improvement for the country. Voting for electors is not equivalent to voting directly for the desired candidate, and in the case of a tie, the House of Representatives offers disproportionate representation, failing to accurately reflect the will and voice of the people. Voters should be entitled to vote and be represented justly, and not be defied and deceived into voting incorrectly or indirectly. Let the people be heard, united in their demand: "Let us Vote." A president who claims to be "For the people" should be chosen "By the people."

Now, the words of a fourteen year old might not mean much to a state senator, especially since it will be four years until I am legally allowed to vote, but please sir, hear me out. I believe that we should keep the Electoral College for many reasons. One, if the president was decided by popular vote, our president might not be the best one for the country. Secondly, if it were up to a popular vote, the president might win, or not win, simply for being a regional favorite.

To start this off, if our president was decided by a popular vote, our president might not be the best thing for our country. As stated in the third article, paragraph 20, "The winnertakeall method of awarding electoral votes induces the candidates... to focus their campaign efforts on the tossup states." As you know, tossup state's make the final vote. Therefore, people in these states are more likely to pay close attention to the campaign, and they will be the ones with the most information. If it were up to popular vote, then anyone over the age of 18 could randomly pick a winner. Let's be honest, the majority of eighteen year old's will vote on whoever their friends or family vote for, or even the candidate representing their group Democratic or Republic. Many people, if the president was decided by popular vote, would make an uneducated decision on one of the candidates, not studying their choices as much as one would with the Electoral College system in place.

Secondly, if it were up to a popular vote, the president might simply win because he or she is a regional favorite. As stated in the third article, paragraph 19, "...because a candidate with only regional appeal is unlikely to be a successful president." Also in the paragraph, it states that no region has enough electoral votes to elect a president. If the president was decided by popular vote, however, can you imagine what it would be like? Take rodney, for example. He was a solid regional favorite in the South. rodney knew that there was no incentive to campaign heavily in the South, because he would not gain any electoral votes by increasing his plurilaty in states he knew he would win. If popularity decided the president, residents of the other regions might feel like their vote would not count, and that the president would have no regard for their interests. Say that popularity did actually determine a winner. While most regions might vote for a certain presidents, the ones that did not would feel like that the man running the country wasnt really "their president". However, Mr. Senator, if the Electoral College was to stay, this problem would never exist. With the system we have now, the people vote for your state's electors when they vote for the president. Most states also have a "winnertakeall" system. Therefore, the canidate with the most votes in a state win's that state's electors. This lets the voting be even, fair. A popular vote would make the voting unfair to those who did not want that president.

Now, of course, the other view has to be adressed. As stated in the second article, "Because of the winnertakeall system in each state, candidates don't spend time in states they know they have no chance of winning... During the 2000 campaign, seventeen states didn't see the candidates at all." This quote shows that the candidates might not go to a state they know they have no chance in winning. I can see how this could support changing to election by popular vote, and how it could be reasonable. If our president was chosen by popular vote, then he would visit all of the states, trying to win them over. However, there are some strong arguments against this. To begin with, one man would have to visit 50 states, and even if he only visited the majority, there is still a posibility that the state will not vote for him anyways, in the end. If we keep the Electoral College, then the majority of votes in a state would count, not his popularity. As a fourteen year old, I can relate this to highschool. A student can be the most popular student in the school, but they can also be the most rude and mean. And while another student may not be popular, they can be the nicest in the school. If the president was chosen by a popular vote, he might just end up being the worst thing that could happen to the country.

In conclusion, Mr. State Senator, I think that keeping the Electoral College is the best thing for our country. If we changed to election by popular vote, the man or woman might not be the best for our country, and the best man might not win because he is not a regional favorite. I hope, sir, that you will take my letter into account when you go to vote on this topic.

While I may be too young to have witnessed much of it, the debate surrounding the Electoral College versus a popular vote election has surely raged for a long time. A state senator might not give much weight to the opinions of a fourteen-year-old, especially considering I won't be eligible to vote for another four years, but I implore you to hear my perspective. I firmly believe the Electoral College should be maintained, and I have several reasons for this conviction. Firstly, a president chosen by popular vote might not truly be the best leader for the nation. Secondly, a popular vote system could lead to a president winning, or losing, based solely on being a regional favorite.

To elaborate, electing our president by popular vote could result in a leader who is detrimental to our country's well-being. As stated in the third article, paragraph 20, "The winner-take-all method of awarding electoral votes induces the candidates... to focus their campaign efforts on the tossup states." These tossup states ultimately determine the election's outcome, meaning their residents are more engaged and informed about the candidates. In contrast, a popular vote system could allow any adult over 18 to cast a vote based on superficial reasons. Let's face it, many young voters might simply choose the candidate favored by their friends or family, or blindly support the representative of their preferred party. With a popular vote, many would make uninformed decisions, lacking the thorough consideration fostered by the Electoral College system.

Furthermore, a popular vote system risks electing a president based solely on regional popularity. The third article, paragraph 19, notes "...because a candidate with only regional appeal is unlikely to be a successful president," also noting that no single region holds enough electoral votes to elect a president alone. Imagine the consequences if popular vote determined the outcome. Consider Rodney, a strong regional favorite in the South. Under the current system, Rodney knows he gains no advantage by over-campaigning in the South, as it won't increase his electoral votes in states he's already sure to win. However, if popularity ruled, voters in other regions might feel their voices are irrelevant, and the president wouldn't prioritize their interests. Even if such a candidate won, those regions that opposed them might feel unrepresented, as if he wasn't "their president." However, retaining the Electoral College avoids this issue. Our current system allows people to vote for state electors when they vote for a president. In most states, the candidate with the most votes wins all of that state's electors. This ensures fair and balanced representation. A popular vote system would unfairly disadvantage those who oppose the winning candidate.

Of course, the opposing viewpoint must be addressed. The second article points out, "Because of the winnertakeall system in each state, candidates don't spend time in states they know they have no chance of winning... During the 2000 campaign, seventeen states didn't see the candidates at all." This suggests candidates neglect states they deem unwinnable. I understand how this could argue for a popular vote system, which would presumably incentivize candidates to campaign nationwide. However, there are valid counterarguments. For one, a candidate would have to visit all 50 states, and even with a majority tour, there's no guarantee of winning votes. Keeping the Electoral College ensures that the majority within a state counts, not just overall popularity. As a high schooler, I see a parallel: the most popular student isn't always the kindest or most virtuous, while a less popular student might be exceptionally caring. Electing a president based solely on popularity could lead to detrimental consequences for our country.

In conclusion, Mr. State Senator, I believe the Electoral College is vital for our nation's future. Shifting to a popular vote system could result in electing a president who isn't the best choice, or who wins simply because of regional appeal. I earnestly hope you will consider my letter when you deliberate on this important issue.

Rewritten Text:
Eliminating the Electoral College would be both illogical and unsound. The founding fathers created this system and enshrined it in the Constitution as a way to reconcile the power of the Congress with that of the populace. While some view it as undemocratic in today's world – given that electors, not individuals directly, choose the president, and a vote for a candidate is actually a vote for a designated group of electors – concerns about electors defying the popular vote are largely unfounded and rare. Therefore, despite claims that it’s undemocratic, the Electoral College should be preserved because it ensures a clear outcome, prevents the need for runoff elections, and rebalances the political influence disproportionately held by larger states.

The possibility of a contested Electoral College result exists, as seen in 2000, but it is less probable than a dispute arising from the popular vote count, which implies that a candidate's share of electoral votes can outweigh their popular vote margin. The 2012 election, where Obama secured 61.7% of the electoral vote compared to 51.3% of the popular vote, exemplifies how the winner-take-all system in nearly all states can create a landslide victory in a state even with a slim popular vote margin.

The Electoral College also prevents elections where no candidate achieves a majority of the vote. In 1968 and 1992, for instance, candidates garnered only a 43% plurality of popular votes, potentially leading to a tied outcome and a runoff election. However, the Electoral College ensures a decisive winner. It's been argued that the Electoral College can deter potential voters in states where their chosen candidate has little chance of winning, such as Democrats in Texas or Republicans in California, suggesting they might not vote if their vote seems inconsequential.

The Electoral College re-establishes some of the political equilibrium lost to larger states due to their population size. In the 2012 election, Florida's close popular vote result and its 29 electoral votes ultimately went to Obama. Thus, larger states tend to receive more attention from presidential candidates during campaigns, as they carry greater electoral weight. While some criticize the winner-take-all system as unfair to voters, candidates rationally focus their efforts on swing states rather than spending time and resources in states with predictable outcomes.

While some fear the Electoral College might discourage voting, as people might perceive their vote as insignificant, no single voter can swing a national election. Presidential election voters are typically motivated by a desire to express a political preference rather than the belief that their individual vote will determine the election's outcome. This reinforces the idea that the Electoral College, not the popular vote, should ultimately determine the nation's leader.

Posner, in "In Defense of the Electoral College: Five reasons to keep our despised method of choosing the President," highlights this disparity. Rewritten Text:
The United States is famed for its guarantees of life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness, alongside the freedoms it offers and the ideal of impartial governance. The electoral college, however, remains a point of contention. This system involves state-selected representatives casting votes for the president, and its fairness has been a recurring debate. Many believe the electoral college is neither equitable nor reliable for several reasons.

Consider a scenario where an elector pledges to support your chosen presidential candidate, only to later alter their vote. This could result in a president whom you actively oppose or distrust. Voters lack complete authority over their electors' decisions, a situation many perceive as unjust. A system of direct popular vote would allow individuals to ensure their preferences are accurately reflected in the election's outcome, rather than potentially being cast against their preferred candidate. History shows instances where candidates won the popular vote yet lost the election through the electoral college, meaning the people's choice did not ultimately secure the presidency. Our foundational rights were hard-earned, and a fair vote is surely among them.

Beyond the issue of potentially misaligned votes, the electoral college introduces other problems. While a tie in a presidential election seems improbable, history reveals instances where the margin between candidates was remarkably slim. The question arises: what would transpire if an election resulted in a complete deadlock? While some might argue the electoral college offers a mechanism to resolve such ties, this is not necessarily true. Given the smaller number of decision-makers involved in the electoral college process, the possibility of a tie could actually be higher. A single elector's shift in allegiance could carry the weight of thousands of individual votes. Moreover, electors often receive rewards when their candidate prevails, despite the widespread support from the people of their state, or even the entire nation, who contributed to their decision-making process, and deserve recognition too.

In conclusion, the electoral college raises serious questions about fairness and trustworthiness. American citizens lack direct control over their electors' votes, and the potential for a disastrous tie is amplified. A commitment to fairness and equal opportunity defines America, and providing its citizens with a fair and direct vote is imperative.

Have you ever considered the long-term consequences of our continued reliance on cars? Think of the air pollution, the damage to our atmosphere, and the depletion of precious resources. We risk creating a future where breathing fresh air becomes impossible without a mask, thanks to pervasive smog. Embracing a "green" approach by reducing car use offers significant benefits for both communities and the planet, leading to cleaner air, more environmentally friendly neighborhoods, and the development of superior transportation alternatives. Once you experience the advantages of a greener lifestyle, there's no turning back.

Smog is a major drawback of car-dependent living, particularly in densely populated areas. Car emissions trapped in the atmosphere cause respiratory problems for many. For instance, Paris, France, had to temporarily ban cars due to record-breaking air pollution levels. This drastic measure, restricting car use on specific days, temporarily alleviated the smog, bringing air quality close to that of Beijing, China, a city notorious for its pollution. Once the smog cleared, the car ban was lifted, essentially returning the city to the same predicament it faces now, given continued car usage.

Furthermore, innovative communities around the globe are emerging where car use is restricted. Vauban, Germany, is one such example, designed to discourage car dependency. A significant 70% of residents don't own a car, and 57% sold their vehicles to move there. As Heidrum Walter, a Vauban resident, notes, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." Similarly, Bogota, Colombia, has implemented programs to reduce car reliance. These car-free lifestyles demonstrate the potential for a better quality of life for communities and individuals.

Finally, while traditional transportation is not ending entirely, new, efficient alternatives are gaining traction. New York City's bike-sharing program has seen a surge in popularity. Carpooling is also becoming more common, reducing the number of vehicles on the road. Studies show that fewer young people in the United States are pursuing driver's licenses and car ownership. These shifts suggest that a world less dependent on cars is within reach.

In conclusion, reducing car dependence is a remarkable step towards a healthier planet. This shift can help us combat air pollution from smog, create greener communities, and promote more efficient transportation methods.

Heidrun Walter, a Vauban resident and media trainer, lives there with her two children and expresses great satisfaction with this lifestyle. Completed in 2006, Vauban represents a growing trend in Europe and the United States, decoupling suburban living from automobile dependence. The community is designed within a rectangular square mile where its 5,500 residents can find all essential services and shops within easy walking distance, concentrated along a central main street.

European nations contribute about 12 percent of global greenhouse gas emissions, while the United States accounts for as much as 50 percent. The impact of these figures became starkly apparent in Paris, where severe smog led to near-record pollution levels and prompted a partial driving ban. The ban restricted vehicles with even-numbered license plates on Mondays and odd-numbered plates on Tuesdays, subject to fines for violations. Despite some driver frustration, the city offered free public transportation throughout the week to ease the burden.

Bogota, Colombia, implemented a national "Day without Cars" to promote car limitations. Millions of Colombians sought alternative transportation to commute to work. The city, home to seven million people, encouraged the use of alternative transportation to combat smog. Mayor Antanas Mockus noted that even rain did not deter participation. During that year’s "Day without Cars," two additional Colombian cities, Cali and Valledupar, joined the event for the first time. According to Enrique Rera, mayor of Asuncin, Paraguay, "These people are generating a revolutionary change, and this is crossing borders." Besides the "Day without Cars," Bogota has also introduced rush-hour restrictions, improved sidewalks, and developed new restaurant and shopping districts.

Whether through Vauban’s comprehensive design for simplified car-free living, Paris’s temporary driving bans, or Bogota’s national transportation alternative day, all three cities offer practical approaches to reducing car use. Globally, individuals appear increasingly ready to embrace changes aimed at minimizing greenhouse gas emissions. In the United States, fewer vehicles are being registered, and the percentage of young people holding driver’s licenses declined by 23 percent between 2001 and 2009. Ultimately, the global goal is to conserve resources, lower greenhouse gas emission rates, and enhance safety, and the current momentum of cities and communities suggests that this objective is attainable.
